narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—pouring your heart into law school dreams only to have them paused like this feels deeply personal, and I'm here to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped, with about a 48% chance of not passing. Picture your profile coming together like chapters in a story: your Year 1 grades landing you in the bottom decile of your school set the opening tone, signaling early struggles that the model flagged as the strongest pull toward difficulty ahead. That pattern held steady by Year 3, with another bottom-decile finish as the second-biggest influence, painting a picture of consistent academic hurdles rather than a quick rebound. Your middle-family income added another layer, as the model links that bracket to tougher odds in high-stakes legal prep compared to higher supports. Even your solid LSAT of 39 and undergrad GPA of 3.4, which are respectable on paper, couldn't fully offset those trends—they nudged toward failure in the model's eyes, especially alongside your background as a white male in a full-time program. It's like the model saw a hardworking guy committed to the grind, but with these threads weaving a narrative of persistent challenges that have tripped up others before you. This isn't about your worth or potential—it's a data-driven snapshot suggesting the bar might demand more lift from someone with your early grade story. Many in similar spots have turned it around by targeting those Year 1 and 3 weaknesses head-on, maybe through extra tutoring or study overhauls. You've got strengths here worth building on, like that LSAT grit, and I truly believe tweaking the script could rewrite your ending. Hang in there; this is just one page, not the whole book.
